5.5 Angstrom Bent] CIS -- 450 nm photon --> S1 CIS -- thermal relaxation half life minutes --> S0 ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Architecture: Photochemical vs Photothermal Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PCH[Photochemical LCN Pathway] P1[UV Photon] --> P2[Azobenzene trans to cis] P2 --> P3[Order Parameter Drops Instantly] P3 --> P4[Mechanical Strain in Seconds] end subgraph PTH[Photothermal LCE Pathway] Q1[Visible or NIR Light] --> Q2[Chromophore Absorbs] Q2 --> Q3[Heat Dissipates into LC] Q3 --> Q4[T rises above T Iso] Q4 --> Q5[Order Parameter Collapses] Q5 --> Q6[Mechanical Strain in Seconds to Minutes] end ``` ## 4.5 Application Map: From Photon to Useful Work ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Photomechanical LC Actuator] --> B[Soft Robotics] A --> C[Microfluidics] A --> D[Biomedical Devices] A --> E[Adaptive Optics] A --> F[Haptic Interfaces] B --> B1[Light Driven Crawlers] B --> B2[Artificial Muscles] C --> C1[Photo Actuated Valves] C --> C2[Self Pumping Chips] D --> D1[Surgical Micro Grippers] D --> D2[Drug Release Capsules] E --> E1[Tunable Lenses] E --> E2[Beam Steering Mirrors] F --> F1[Refreshable Braille Displays] F --> F2[Texture Changing Surfaces] ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a photomechanical liquid crystal actuator. Mention any two key advantages of using light as the stimulus over heat or electric field.** **Model Answer:** A photomechanical liquid crystal actuator is a stimuli-responsive soft device in which incident photons induce a reversible shape change in photoactive mesogens (commonly azobenzene), which is amplified through the cooperative orientational order and elastic coupling of a liquid crystal polymer network (LCN) or elastomer (LCE) into a macroscopic mechanical deformation such as bending, contraction, or twisting. Two key advantages of light as the stimulus: 1. **Remote and contactless actuation** — light can be delivered through free space or optical fibers, eliminating the need for wires, electrodes, or direct physical contact. This enables operation in sealed, sterile, or otherwise inaccessible environments such as inside the human body or vacuum chambers. 2. **Spatial and temporal precision** — light can be patterned using masks, scanners, or focused beams, allowing *addressed* actuation of specific regions of the actuator with millisecond switching. Heat and electric fields are difficult to confine to small volumes without cross-talk. *(Any other valid advantage — e.g. wavelength selectivity, no Joule heating, compatibility with biological tissue — accepted for full marks.)* ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the role of azobenzene in a photomechanical LC actuator. Why is its *trans–cis* photoisomerization considered the molecular "engine" of the device?** **Model Answer:** Azobenzene is the photoactive chromophore responsible for converting photons into molecular shape change in an LC actuator. Under ultraviolet light (~365 nm), the planar *trans* form (rod-like, ~9 Å) photoisomerizes into the bent *cis* form (~5.5 Å) by rotation about the central N=N double bond. Under visible light (~450 nm) or spontaneously in the dark at room temperature, the *cis* form reverts back to *trans*. It is called the molecular "engine" because: 1. **Large and reversible geometry change** — the ~40 % contraction along the long axis of the molecule provides a substantial mechanical impulse per absorbed photon. 2. **High fatigue resistance** — azobenzene can undergo >10⁴ isomerization cycles without chemical degradation, ensuring long actuator lifetime. 3. **Coupling to LC order** — when tethered as a side-chain mesogen in an aligned network, the rod-to-bend transition of one azobenzene reduces the local order parameter *S*. Because of the cooperative nature of the LC phase, this single-molecule event propagates to many neighbors, and the cross-linked network translates the drop in *S* into a macroscopic contraction along the director. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Module 4 Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat energy-level diagram, describe the *trans–cis* photoisomerization of azobenzene. Discuss the factors that govern the forward and reverse quantum yields. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how the photoisomerization of azobenzene leads to a macroscopic bending deformation in a monodomain azobenzene LCN cantilever. Derive an expression for the tip bending angle in terms of the photogenerated strain $\varepsilon_{\parallel}$ and the cantilever geometry. #### Model Solution to (a): **Energy-level diagram and isomerization mechanism:** Azobenzene exists in two geometric isomers — *trans* (E) and *cis* (Z). The photoisomerization proceeds through excited electronic states. - *Trans* azobenzene has its ground state $S_0$ populated at room temperature. Absorption of a UV photon at ~365 nm promotes the molecule to the first excited singlet state $S_1(n,\pi^{*})$, in which the N=N bond becomes rotationally labile. The molecule rotates about the N=N bond and relaxes into the *cis* ground state geometry. - *Cis* azobenzene, on absorption of a visible photon (~450 nm), is excited to $S_1(\pi,\pi^{*})$ and rotates back to the *trans* geometry. - A *thermal* pathway from *cis* → *trans* also exists in the dark, with activation energy $\approx 50\,\text{kJ/mol}$ and a half-life of minutes to hours. **Factors governing quantum yields:** 1. **Excitation wavelength** — the quantum yield of *trans* → *cis* is wavelength dependent; excitation at the n→π* band (~450 nm, from *trans*) gives a higher isomerization yield per absorbed photon than excitation at the π→π* band (~320 nm) because the $S_1(n,\pi^{*})$ state is directly dissociative along the rotation coordinate. 2. **Substituents** — electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups at the para positions (*push-pull* azobenzenes) shift absorption into the visible range and can enhance the quantum yield. 3. **Local environment** — in a polymer matrix or LC host, the free volume available for rotation limits the yield; high cross-link density typically *reduces* the quantum yield. 4. **Temperature** — higher temperatures accelerate the thermal *cis* → *trans* back-reaction, effectively lowering the steady-state *cis* fraction. A typical *trans* → *cis* quantum yield is $\Phi_{t \to c} \approx 0.2\text{–}0.4$, while $\Phi_{c \to t} \approx 0.4\text{–}0.6$ under visible light. #### Model Solution to (b): **Mechanism of bending in a monodomain azobenzene LCN cantilever:** A monodomain azobenzene LCN is a thin film in which the LC director **n** is uniformly aligned, typically by photo-alignment or rubbed polyimide. Azobenzene mesogens are covalently tethered as side-chains. Under UV irradiation, *trans* → *cis* isomerization in the azobenzene units reduces the local order parameter $S$. Because the cis form is bent and sterically incompatible with the nematic order, it acts as an "impurity" that disrupts the surrounding mesogens. The result is a contraction of the network along the director **n** and an expansion perpendicular to **n**. In a thin cantilever film, the UV light is strongly absorbed within the first few hundred nanometers from the top surface (Beer–Lambert law, $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$). Therefore, the top layer contracts more than the bottom layer, generating an internal bending moment — analogous to a bimetallic strip with two metals of different thermal expansion coefficients. **Derivation of the tip bending angle:** Consider a cantilever of length $L$ and uniform thickness $t$, with uniform axial strain $\varepsilon_{\parallel}$ in the active layer of thickness $t_a$. The strain mismatch between the active (top) layer and the passive (bottom) layer produces a curvature: $$\frac{1}{R} \;=\; \frac{6\,\varepsilon_{\parallel}\,(1-m)^2}{t\,\left[3\,(1-m)^2 + (1+mn)\,(m^2 + \tfrac{1}{mn})\right]}$$ where $m = t_a / t$ is the thickness fraction and $n = E_a / E_p$ is the elastic modulus ratio. For a homogeneous single-layer film ($m = 1$, $n = 1$), this simplifies to: $$\frac{1}{R} \;\approx\; \frac{6\,\varepsilon_{\parallel}}{t}$$ The tip deflection and tip angle are then: $$\delta_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{3\,L^2\,\varepsilon_{\parallel}}{t}, \qquad \alpha_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{3\,L\,\varepsilon_{\parallel}}{t}$$ For example, with $L = 5\,\text{mm}$, $t = 10\,\mu\text{m}$, $\varepsilon_{\parallel} = 0.20$, we get: $$\alpha_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 5\times10^{-3} \times 0.20}{10\times10^{-6}} \;=\; 300\,\text{rad}$$ Such a large angle means the film curls into a multi-turn helix rather than bending gently. Experimentally, this is exactly what is observed for thin azobenzene LCN films under UV light. --- ### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Module 4 Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare photochemical and photothermal mechanisms of actuation in liquid crystal polymers. Discuss the wavelength of operation, response time, and typical strain achievable in each case. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe with examples any three applications of photomechanical LC actuators. Discuss the materials design considerations that make these actuators suitable for biomedical applications. #### Model Solution to (a): **Comparison between photochemical and photothermal LC actuators:** | Parameter | Photochemical LCN | Photothermal LCE | |---|---|---| | Operating wavelength | UV (~365 nm) | Visible / NIR (400–900 nm) | | Mechanism | Direct *trans ↔ cis* isomerization reduces *S* | Light absorbed as heat raises *T* above *T*ᵢ, reducing *S* | | Response time | Seconds | Seconds to minutes (limited by thermal diffusion) | | Typical strain | ~10–20 % | ~50–400 % | | Reversibility trigger | Visible light (~450 nm) | Cooling below *T*ᵢ | | Energy per cycle | Single photon per molecule | Multiple photons (heat whole sample) | | Biocompatibility concern | UV hazard to cells | Mostly safe (visible/NIR) | | Fatigue | >10⁴ cycles | >10³ cycles (thermal cycling) | **Discussion:** - In **photochemical LCNs**, every absorbed UV photon directly converts one *trans* azobenzene to *cis*. The strain is limited by the maximum achievable cis fraction (~80 % at photostationary state) and by the cross-link density that prevents full network collapse. Typical strains are 10–20 %. - In **photothermal LCEs**, light is absorbed by a dye or plasmonic nanoparticle, raising the local temperature. Once *T* > *T*ᵢ (the LC–isotropic transition temperature), the order parameter *S* drops to zero, and the network contracts maximally. Strains can reach 100–400 % because the LC phase is fully disrupted. - The **response time** of photochemical systems is limited by the isomerization rate (~picoseconds per molecule, but full sample conversion requires seconds due to photon flux). Photothermal systems are limited by thermal diffusion: $\tau \sim L^2 / D_{th}$, which for a 50 µm film is on the order of 10 ms. - A **hybrid** approach uses push-pull azobenzenes or upconversion nanoparticles to drive photochemistry with visible/NIR light, combining the speed of photochemistry with the deeper tissue penetration of NIR. #### Model Solution to (b): **Three applications of photomechanical LC actuators:** 1. **Light-driven micro-grippers for minimally invasive surgery.** A photoresponsive LCN gripper can be inserted into the body in a closed configuration through a small incision, then opened under endoscopic UV illumination to grasp a tissue sample, drug-delivery capsule, or stent. Light provides wireless control without physical tethers. 2. **Self-cleaning and adaptive surfaces.** A photomechanical LCN film coated on a window or solar panel can shed dust and contaminants by periodically bending or vibrating under sunlight. The same principle is used in self-cleaning camera lenses and aerospace windows. 3. **Photo-tunable optical elements.** Azobenzene LCNs are used as tunable diffraction gratings, beam-steering mirrors, and variable-focal-length lenses. The surface relief or curvature changes under patterned illumination, enabling adaptive optics without mechanical motors. **Materials design considerations for biomedical applications:** - **Operating wavelength:** Must be shifted from UV (which damages DNA and proteins) into the visible or NIR ("optical window" 650–950 nm) using push-pull azobenzenes, two-photon chromophores, or upconversion nanoparticles. - **Biocompatibility:** The polymer must be non-toxic and non-immunogenic. Common choices are PEG-functionalized monomers and biodegradable cross-linkers. - **Mechanical compliance:** The modulus must match soft tissue (~kPa to MPa). LCNs are typically ~1–10 MPa, comparable to muscle, but softer elastomers can be tuned to <100 kPa. - **Degradation products:** Any hydrolysis or photo-oxidation products must be metabolically cleared. Acrylate-based networks degrade to poly(acrylic acid), which is biologically tolerated. - **Sterilizability:** The actuator must survive autoclaving, gamma irradiation, or ethanol treatment without losing photomechanical performance. - **Cycle life:** Tens of thousands of cycles are required for chronic implants. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully** > Students frequently lose 3–5 marks on photomechanical actuator questions because of the following mistakes: > 1. **Confusing photochemical and photothermal pathways.** UV light + azobenzene ⇒ *photochemical*. Visible/NIR + heating ⇒ *photothermal*. Mixing the two is a guaranteed half-mark deduction per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting the role of the cross-linker.** Without covalent cross-links, the polymer would simply flow under strain. Always mention cross-link density as a design parameter. > 3. **Writing only the magnitude of strain.** The KTU answer key explicitly awards marks for the *direction* of strain (parallel contraction, perpendicular expansion) and the *sign convention*. > 4. **Skipping the quantum yield / photostationary state argument.** A 7-mark question on azobenzene photochemistry that omits the quantum yield will be capped at 4 marks. > 5. **Not drawing the bending geometry.** A neat sketch of the cantilever with the director marked earns an easy 2 marks even if the derivation is incomplete. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Photomechanical LC actuator** = photoactive chromophore + aligned LC mesogen + cross-linked network. - **Azobenzene is the canonical photoactive unit.** *Trans* (UV, ~365 nm) ↔ *cis* (Vis, ~450 nm or thermal). - **The photochemical strain is governed by $\varepsilon_{\parallel} \approx -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\Delta S / S_0$** — a smaller initial $S_0$ gives larger strain for a given $\Delta S$. - **Photochemical LCNs** act on the order parameter directly via UV; **photothermal LCEs** act via heating above $T_{\text{iso}}$ using visible/NIR. - **Bending of a cantilever** follows $\alpha_{\text{tip}} \approx 3\,L\,\varepsilon_{\parallel}/t$. Thin, long films curl more. - **Photothermal time constant** scales as $\tau \sim L^2/D_{\text{th}}$. Thin films respond in milliseconds. - **Design levers**: chromophore concentration, cross-link density, alignment quality, film thickness, push-pull substitution. - **Applications**: soft robotics, surgical micro-grippers, adaptive optics, microfluidic pumps, haptic displays, self-cleaning surfaces. - **Biomedical use** requires shifting excitation to the NIR optical window, biocompatible monomers, and modulus matching to soft tissue. - **Always state** the operating wavelength, the mechanism (photochemical vs photothermal), the sign of strain, and the reversibility trigger in any exam answer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain photomechanical LC actuators," always structure your answer in four layers — (i) molecular photoisomerization of azobenzene, (ii) reduction of order parameter *S*, (iii) coupling to the cross-linked network producing anisotropic strain, and (iv) macroscopic deformation (bending, twisting, crawling). Examiners reward answers that progress systematically from the molecular to the macroscopic scale.
